protocol: medical - respiratory distress/asthma/copd/croup/reactive airway (respiratory distress - croup/epiglottits) (protocol 9 - 11)

dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4, responding for breathing problems at Richmo and 8th.
Hello, my name is Jordan and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having a hard time breathing, it's been going on for a few days now.
Amanda, can you tell me where you are and the date today?
I'm at my house, and I'm not really sure of the date.
That's okay-what's your name again?
My name is Amanda.
We're going to listen to your lungs now. Could you take a deep breath for us?
Lungs are mostly clear except for some wheezing in the right lower lobe. SpO2 is 99% in room air.
What other medications are you currently taking besides your inhaler?
I've been using my albuterol a lot, but it's not helping.
Have you had any other health issues besides asthma?
I have eczema and occasional breathing problems, but not much else.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst possible shortness of breath, how would you rate it?
Maybe a 7. It's tough when I talk or move around.
You heard wheezing in the right lower lobe. Can you feel it elsewhere in your chest?
Slightly in my back, but mainly on the right side.
I notice a minor increase in your work of breathing - we'll monitor that.
Pulse is 128.
Breathing started at 19 but is now at 24.
Blood pressure was 112 over 73, and it's now 143 over 109.
Oxygen saturation is now 98%, just slightly down from the initial 99%.
The glucose monitor showed 0, but it was unclear. We're treating this as unknown for now.
The EKG shows sinus tachycardia with heart rates of 128 and 138 reported.
We're giving DuoNeb, a nebulizer mix of Albuterol and Atrovent, as well as Dexamethasone 10mg intravenously.
We started an IV in your left arm and are monitoring EKG for tachycardia tracking under the respiratory distress protocol.
I see signs of eczema, but no active rash or scarring at this moment.
You're alert and oriented. GCS score is 15 - eye opening 4, verbal 5, motor 6. No concerns from the neurological standpoint.
We are following protocol 9-11 for asthma exacerbation, based on labored breathing and the minimal response to your inhaler.
Pulse has increased slightly to 138 with no changes in rhythm - still sinus tachycardia.
Respiration rate remains at 24 breaths per minute and is slightly labored but well-tolerated.
Blood pressure is stable at 143 over 109.
First dose of the nebulizer med was given, and the patient said she feels the wheezing is easing.
IV is still in good condition, and EKG remains clear with consistent tachycardia.
Using the 0 to 10 scale again, how do you feel now in terms of your breathing?
A little better - around a 4 now. I can sit and talk more easily.
We'll be transporting you to St. Mary's Hospital for the pediatric ED. Your mom will be with us during the ride, okay?
Equipment is all secured. IV continues, EKG is stable, and medications were administered properly - ready for transport.
Vitals remain stable: BP is 143/109, heart rate is 138, and breathing 24. Capnography shows a normal trend in ventilation during initial meds.
Completed second nebulizer treatment. She now breathes more easily, and the sensation is improved.
I noticed you mentioned no pain earlier. Is breathing the only struggle you are having at this moment?
When did you last have something to eat or drink?
Around 2 hours ago, some cereal with orange juice for breakfast.
On that 0 to 10 scale again, how would you rate it now?
A 3. I can walk and talk without much trouble - definitely improving.
We're all set. Thanks so much for helping her.
Mid-transport vitals remain the same. Only small oxygen variations noted, nothing to be alarmed about.
Capnography and nebulizer setups are working well. Everything is in stable condition for transport.
All protocol steps were followed and confirmed. Vitals are stable, IV is active, and EKG condition is consistent.
Arrived at St. Mary's Hospital. Patient is in good condition and has been transferred into the Pediatric ED for follow-up.
